turns.content: Hey, I'm having trouble understanding the difference between #[wasm_bindgen] and web-sys when I'm trying to call a JavaScript function from my Rust WASM module. Which one should I actually use?

Hey! Great question. The #[wasm_bindgen] macro is the core entry point that tells wasm-bindgen how to bridge your Rust code with JavaScript, while web-sys provides a set of types that mirror browser APIs like DOM elements or Window objects for type safety. You absolutely need #[wasm_bindgen] on every function you want to expose to JS, but you typically use web-sys inside your Rust code if you're accessing browser features directly without going through JavaScript first.
Okay, so I have a function that needs to return a complex object from JS. Should I use JsValue or just regular types like Vec<String>?

If you're returning arbitrary JavaScript objects or values that don't map cleanly to Rust primitives, you should wrap them in JsValue using #[wasm_bindgen] with the appropriate attributes. However, if your return type is something like Vec<String> or a simple struct that implements Serialize, you can usually just use those native Rust types directly and wasm-bindgen will handle the conversion automatically.
I'm getting an error saying I can't return my struct. It seems like I need to manually construct JsValue instances with values()

That's a common mistake when you're first diving into this. You don't necessarily need to manually create JsValue instances unless your Rust type doesn't implement the necessary traits or isn't directly convertible. If your struct has a Serialize implementation, wasm-bindgen should handle it for you. The error suggests you might be trying to return something that can't be serialized yet.
Wait, I think I misunderstood how the conversion works. Can't I just use JsValue::from_serde() on my struct and then wrap it in a #[wasm_bindgen] function?

You're very close! JsValue::from_serde is useful for converting arbitrary serde values to JavaScript, but you don't typically use it inside the #[wasm_bindgen] macro itself. Instead, you'd implement Serialize on your Rust struct and let wasm-bindgen do the heavy lifting of conversion when you return that struct from a function.
Ah okay, so I should just add #[serde] to my struct? But what about bundling this with wasm-pack? Does it handle all these conversions automatically?

wasm-pack will bundle your project and generate the necessary JavaScript glue code, but you still need to manually add #[serde] attributes to your Rust structs if they contain complex data. wasm-pack doesn't magically convert everything for you—you do need to tell it what types are serializable.
Got it, so the workflow is: define struct with #[serde], add #[wasm_bindgen] to function returning that struct, and wasm-pack handles the rest?

Exactly! That's the standard pattern. Define your Rust types with proper serde attributes, expose them through #[wasm_bindgen] functions, then run wasm-pack build to generate the JavaScript bindings and bundle everything together.
One last thing—what if I need to call a specific JS function that returns an object? Do I use web-sys::HTMLElement or just #[wasm_bindgen]?

For calling arbitrary JavaScript functions from Rust, you should use #[wasm_bindgen] with the js_name attribute to specify which function in your JS file it corresponds to. If you need types like HTMLElement or Window for your Rust code logic, those come from web-sys and you'd import them at the top of your module.

I'm trying to get back into Rust after five years and I'm stuck on a code review. My colleague wrote some WASM bindings using #[wasm_bindgen] but they're returning JsValue instead of concrete types like String or i32, which makes it hard to type-check later.

That is actually a very common mistake when first diving into wasm-bindgen. When you return JsValue directly from a #[wasm_bindgen] function without specifying the concrete Rust type, the caller has to do manual parsing and unwrapping of the JsValue enum variants before they can safely use the data.

Exactly. They have me manually inspecting JsValue::from_str or checking if it's a String variant every time I call their function, which feels like an anti-pattern compared to standard Rust return types.

You are right that this defeats the purpose of safe FFI. The wasm-bindgen crate is designed so that #[wasm_bindgen] functions can declare explicit output types like String or i32, which allows Rust to automatically handle the conversion from JsValue via web-sys and provide proper type safety without manual inspection.
So if I change their return statement to use concrete types, does wasm-pack still bundle it correctly?

Yes, wasm-pack will bundle it correctly because the #[wasm_bindgen] macro generates the necessary glue code for those specific output types. However, you should also check if they are using JsCast to convert values when calling into JS functions from Rust, since web-sys often requires explicit casting when bridging direction changes.

Why does this even work this way? I'm trying to call a JavaScript function from Rust but it feels like magic. The docs say #[wasm_bindgen] and JsValue but I can't figure out how the types actually map.

It's not really magic; wasm-bindgen generates the glue code at compile time using macros to create FFI boundaries. When you annotate a function with #[wasm_bindgen], it inserts runtime logic that handles type conversion and memory management so Rust can call into JS without manual pointer manipulation.

Okay, but I'm getting an error about JsValue. It seems like my function is expecting a specific return type but the macro is giving me something else. Is this a limitation of how wasm-pack bundles things?

That's not a bundling issue; it's about what you're declaring in your Rust function signature. JsValue is the generic wrapper for any JS value, but if your JS function returns a string or number, wasm-bindgen can infer that and give you more specific types like &str or f64 instead of forcing you to handle raw JsValue manually.
I see. So I should be avoiding #[wasm_bindgen] for everything? Or is there a time when I really need the generic wrapper?

You only use JsValue directly when you're dealing with polymorphic JS values that can't be statically inferred, like dynamic arrays or objects. For most cases, wasm-bindgen's type inference on functions is sufficient and safer because it prevents runtime panics from bad type conversions.
Got it. But what if I need to pass a complex Rust struct with nested fields? Does the macro handle that automatically or do I have to write extra glue code?

The #[wasm_bindgen] attribute handles most of the serialization for you, but if your struct has non-serializable fields like untracked pointers, you'll need to mark those with #[cfg_attr(target_arch = "wasm32", derive(Debug))] or use explicit conversions. wasm-pack's default config usually works fine unless you're doing something very low-level.
I'm still confused about the memory management part. When I call a JS function, does Rust actually allocate heap memory for return values or is it all just references?

wasm-bindgen uses reference semantics by default; it doesn't automatically allocate heap memory for simple types unless you explicitly use JsValue::new(). For primitive conversions, the runtime creates temporary objects on the JS side and returns a reference back to Rust without extra allocations.
So performance-wise, is there overhead compared to writing raw C FFI bindings manually?

Yes, wasm-bindgen has a small runtime cost because it needs to check types and manage the JS->Rust boundary at every call site. But for most applications this overhead is negligible compared to the complexity of writing manual C FFI bindings in Rust.
Alright, one last thing. If I want to expose a large array from JS back to Rust, does wasm-bindgen support that or do I need to write custom code?

You can use JsValue::from_vec() to pass vectors directly, but for large arrays you might want to consider using web-sys's TypedArray bindings or implementing a custom conversion trait. wasm-pack handles basic serialization well, but large data structures often benefit from explicit type annotations.
